entry.title: Bitcoin covenants are inevitable
entry.summary:
The discussion revolves around the critical issue of establishing optimal security measures for Bitcoin, especially as the block subsidy diminishes over time. The current model suggests a feedback loop where higher Bitcoin prices could justify increased investment in security. However, concerns are raised about the long-term sustainability of this link as subsidies become less significant. This scenario necessitates a thorough consensus on security investment needs, ideally within the next decade or so, to prepare adequately.
A recent paper ([quantification of Consensus Protocol Security](https://github.com/fresheneesz/quantificationOfConsensusProtocolSecurity)) introduces a framework designed to evaluate the necessary security levels for Bitcoin. It explores potential bottlenecks and sets preliminary goals regarding the minimum operational requirements for nodes within the network. These considerations are based on an estimation of how many public nodes are needed and the likelihood of machines available to run these nodes. The findings and methodologies presented in the paper are open for debate, emphasizing the need for community engagement in refining these security parameters.
The proposal further suggests that adjustments to Bitcoin's block size could serve as a mechanism to modulate security investments. By altering the block size, miners' fee revenues per block are affected, which in turn influences the overall security funding. The relationship between block size and fees is not straightforward but rather forms a parabolic trajectory, suggesting there exists an optimal block size that maximizes fee revenue without over-investing in security. Periodic adjustments to the block size, possibly through software updates or forks, are recommended to maintain security investment close to this optimal point.
Moreover, the lack of a trustless system to ascertain Bitcoin’s market value poses challenges in maintaining accurate security targets. Transaction fees might offer some insights into market valuation, but more robust mechanisms or analyses will be necessary to enhance prediction accuracy. The idea of blocks committing to a market price, similar to how they commit to timestamps, is suggested as a potential approach, though it comes with its own set of limitations and would require close temporal verification.
